title: Meridian — should we raise a $40M Series B to go upmarket?
subject: Meridian Health Software
decision: Should Meridian raise a $40M Series B to fund a move upmarket into DSOs (enterprise product + enterprise sales team), or is there a higher-leverage path given the current numbers?
situation:
Meridian Health Software — vertical SaaS (practice-management + scheduling + billing) for independent dental practices.

Facts available:
- ARR $14.2M today; YoY growth decelerated from ~90% (two years ago) to 28% now, slowing each of the last 4 quarters.
- 2,400 customers, almost all 1–3 chair independent practices; average ACV ~$5,900.
- Net revenue retention 96% today, down from 112% eighteen months ago. Gross logo churn 14%/yr and rising.
- New-customer CAC up 38% over the last 12 months; CAC payback now 19 months (was 11). Sales-led motion, 18 AEs; win rate fell from 32% to 24%.
- A PE-backed competitor that owns a dental-insurance product is bundling a free practice-management system to win logos.
- Two attempts last year to sell upmarket to DSOs (dental service organizations — multi-location groups) failed: both DSO pilots churned within 6 months citing missing multi-location reporting and no SSO.
- Cash: 14 months of runway; 95 employees.

The board is split on the path forward.

vars.decision: Should Meridian raise a $40M Series B to fund a move upmarket into DSOs (enterprise product + enterprise sales team), or is there a higher-leverage path given the current numbers?

note: A vertical-SaaS Series B decision — exercises competitive substitution, NRR collapse, and an upmarket product-gap thesis.
